bio.title: Reverend
bio.photo_notes: Reverend Asahel Clark Kendrick, DD LL.D. (1809-1895), inaugural professor of Greek at University of Rochester

burial.plot_notes: Mount Hope Cemetery Rochester per FA — alongside H02025 Helen MacNaughton Hooker Kendrick

notable.notable_blurb: First professor of Greek at University of Rochester

education.notes: First American professor to study at the University of Athens.

career.role: Professor of Ancient and Modern Languages
career.organization: Madison University (now Colgate University), Hamilton, New York
career.notes: Also designed the university seal in 1846.

career.role: Inaugural Professor of Greek Language and Literature (Munro Professorship)
career.notes: Also: Chair of Classics 1850-95; First Librarian 1850-66; Acting President 1863 and 1877-78; Acting Professor of Biblical Literature & NT 1865-68 and 1875-77.

career.role: Acting Professor of Biblical Literature and New Testament
career.notes: Concurrent with U of R duties.

career.role: Member, American New Testament Revision Committee
career.notes: Contributed to the Revised Version of 1881.

institutions.institution_blurb: Graduated Hamilton College 1831; immediately joined faculty as tutor in classics.
Inaugural Munro Professor of Greek 1850-1888; first librarian 1850-1866; acting president 1863, 1877-78. Kendrick Hall named in his honor, 1913.

narrative_blocks.header: First American professor to study Greek in Athens
narrative_blocks.body: In 1852, Asahel Clark Kendrick traveled to Greece and attended lectures at the University of Athens, becoming among the first American professors to study in the country whose language he had spent his career teaching — a journey motivated partly by health concerns and partly by the conviction that direct immersion in classical origins would transform his pedagogy. He also visited universities in Italy and Germany, and returned to Rochester in 1854 with continental insights that shaped his methods for the next three decades and found expression in his 1855 volume Echoes: or Leisure Hours with the German Poets.

narrative_blocks.header: Ordained minister who never held a single pastorate
narrative_blocks.body: Kendrick was ordained as a Baptist minister but chose the classroom over the pulpit, spending sixty years as a professor rather than a pastor at Madison University and the University of Rochester — the longest academic career in that institution's early history — without ever accepting a congregation. His father Clark Kendrick had been a Baptist missionary and minister in Vermont; the son transformed the same conviction into an academic vocation, applying it to the study of Greek grammar, biblical exegesis, and classical philology.

narrative_blocks.header: The first dormitory was named for him posthumously
narrative_blocks.body: Kendrick Hall, the University of Rochester's first men's dormitory, was dedicated in January 1913 — eighteen years after Kendrick's death — to honor the man who had been among the university's three founding faculty members in 1850 and had served as its acting president, first librarian, and Munro Professor of Greek for thirty-eight years. His daughter Florence Hopkins Kendrick Cooper published a biographical tribute that same year: An American Scholar: A Tribute to Asahel Clark Kendrick, 1809-1895.

narrative_blocks.header: Presided over the American Philological Association, 1872
narrative_blocks.body: Elected president of the American Philological Association in 1872 and delivering the presidential address in 1873 on the relations of philology to national culture, Kendrick used the platform to champion Greek and Latin as essential to American moral and intellectual development at a moment when classical curricula were under growing modern pressure. He was also elected an associate fellow of the American Academy of Arts and Sciences in 1866 and served on the American New Testament Revision Committee from 1872 to 1880, contributing to the Revised Version of 1881.

sources.label: Rutgers Dictionary of Baptists in America — Kendrick entry
Florence Kendrick Cooper, An American Scholar (New York, 1913)

burial.plot_notes: Mount Hope Cemetery Rochester — buried with husband Asahel Clark Kendrick

narrative_blocks.header: Died at fifty-three, survived by her professor husband
narrative_blocks.body: Helen MacNaughton Hooker Kendrick died on November 1, 1880 at age fifty-three — exactly fifteen years before her husband, Professor Asahel Clark Kendrick, died in the same Rochester home at age eighty-five after sixty years of teaching Greek at the university he had helped found in 1850. She and Kendrick were buried together at Mount Hope Cemetery, where he joined her in 1895, the dormitory that would bear his name not to be dedicated for another eighteen years.

narrative_blocks.header: Her marriage linked Hartford's Puritans to Rochester's founders
narrative_blocks.body: When Helen MacNaughton Hooker married Asahel Clark Kendrick in 1857, she connected the descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker of Hartford — the founding Puritan of Connecticut — to the man who had helped establish the University of Rochester seven years earlier and would teach Greek there for thirty-eight years. The union meant that two of Kendrick's children by Helen (Ryland and Josephine) were both Hooker descendants and heirs to a Rochester academic legacy, a thread that would run through four generations to Ann Kendrick McNabb in the twenty-first century.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)
Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)
Alexander Hooker Papers, D.344 — University of Rochester Special Collections

naming_inspiration.description: Named for his dead half-brother Alexander Allin Hooker (b.1763; d.1781; FAG 22532503), son of Capt. James Hooker's second wife Hannah Allin — a memorial naming that also honors the Allin family name carried through Capt. James's second wife.

military_service.unit: Unknown — service documented in son's obituary
military_service.notes: Fought at Battle of Lundy's Lane (July 25, 1814) and Battle of Queenston Heights (Oct 13, 1812). Documented in obituary of his son Capt. James Henry Hooker, Rochester newspaper, March 1907: 'His father, Alexander B. Hooker, served in the war of 1812, fighting in the battles of Lundy's Lane, Queenstown, and others.'

burial.plot_notes: Hooker Cemetery — land he deeded to Irondequoit in 1840; family plot survived 1950s accidental destruction

narrative_blocks.header: Pioneer settler who gave Irondequoit its Hooker Cemetery
narrative_blocks.body: Alexander Allin Hooker arrived in the Rochester area in 1816 — with his brother Horace — to visit their sister Dolly (Dolly Goodwin Hooker Strong, who had recently married and settled in Canandaigua) and stayed to open a general store that lasted three years; by 1828 he had purchased Jesse Tainter's farm and by 1839 he was Irondequoit's first Town Clerk, and in 1840 he deeded land that became Hooker Cemetery to the town. His family burial plot there is one of the few in the cemetery to survive intact when the town accidentally destroyed most of the grounds in the 1950s, leaving his stone among the more legible remnants of the family that gave Irondequoit its first civic institutions.

narrative_blocks.header: Fought at Lundy's Lane before founding Irondequoit
narrative_blocks.body: Before settling in the Rochester area, Alexander Allin Hooker served in the War of 1812, fighting at the battles of Lundy's Lane (July 25, 1814, one of the bloodiest engagements on the Canadian border) and Queenston Heights — service documented in his son's 1907 newspaper obituary, which called him 'a soldier' who 'served in the war of 1812, fighting in the battles of Lundy's Lane, Queenstown, and others.' He came to Rochester in 1816, the year after the war ended, and within two decades had deeded a cemetery and founded a town — a veteran who became a civic patriarch.

narrative_blocks.header: An old Indian trail passed behind his cemetery
narrative_blocks.body: The burying ground Alexander Hooker made public in 1840 sat on a sandy knoll traversed by an Old Indian Trail that ran from the Seneca Village on Boughton Hill (near Victor, New York) to the Indian Landing at the mouth of the Genesee River — meaning the Hooker Cemetery was not merely a settler burial ground but sat astride an ancient Seneca thoroughfare, and likely contains Seneca burials alongside those of the earliest European settlers. It is estimated that perhaps a thousand people are buried in the one-and-a-half acres, most without markers, interred by pioneers under pine and oak trees before the ground was formally enclosed.

burial.plot_notes: Hooker Cemetery Irondequoit — inscription: "She acted well her part and sleeps in Jesus"

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)
